Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Respiratory Medicine digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 15 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Respiratory Medicine.

15articles that week
4journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Chest

Effects of antifungal treatment in COVID-19-associated pulmonary aspergillosis: A European multicenter cohort study

Reizine F, Feys S, Seidel D et al. · 2026 Apr 17
Study Type: Multicenter retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Is antifungal treatment associated with lower 60-day mortality in patients with probable or proven COVID-19-associated pulmonary aspergillosis (CAPA)?
Key Findings:
  • Among 259 CAPA patients, antifungal treatment was associated with significantly reduced 60-day mortality (weighted HR 0.28, 95% CI: 0.13-0.58, p<0.001)
  • 91.5% received antifungals (mostly azoles), with baseline characteristics similar between treated and untreated groups
  • Age, immunosuppressive treatment, and remdesivir were independently associated with increased mortality, while male sex and antifungal treatment reduced mortality risk
Clinical Relevance: This provides the largest evidence base to date supporting antifungal treatment for CAPA, reinforcing the importance of active screening and early treatment in UK ICUs managing COVID-19 patients, particularly immunocompromised individuals.
Limitations: Retrospective design with potential unmeasured confounding factors affecting treatment allocation decisions.
PubMed →DOI →
Chest

Prognostic Significance of Mild Exacerbations or One Moderate Exacerbation in COPD: A Prospective Community-Based Cohort Study

Wu F, Wan Q, Tian H et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Prospective community-based cohort study
Key Question: Are mild exacerbations or a single moderate exacerbation associated with worse respiratory outcomes in community-dwelling COPD patients?
Key Findings:
  • Patients with only mild exacerbations (4% of cohort) had 67% higher risk of future total exacerbations (RR 1.67, 95% CI 1.11-2.51) and 76% higher risk of moderate-severe exacerbations compared to those with no exacerbations
  • Those with single moderate exacerbation (5% of cohort) had 89% higher risk of total exacerbations (RR 1.89, 95% CI 1.31-2.73) and 129% higher risk of moderate-severe exacerbations
  • Both groups showed more severe CT-defined emphysema and air trapping but no difference in lung function decline rates
Clinical Relevance: This challenges the assumption that infrequent or mild exacerbations are benign, suggesting UK respiratory services should monitor and potentially optimise treatment for COPD patients even after single moderate or mild exacerbations.
Limitations: Community-based recruitment may not represent the more severe COPD population typically seen in secondary care.
PubMed →DOI →
Chest

Patient Perceptions of Quality of Shared Decision-Making for Lung Cancer Screening in Telehealth versus In-Person Discussions

Robinson SA, Shusterman S, Barker AM et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Cross-sectional survey study
Key Question: How does telehealth versus in-person delivery affect patient-perceived quality of shared decision-making for lung cancer screening?
Key Findings:
  • No significant difference in perceived SDM quality between telehealth and in-person consultations (adjusted OR 0.76, 95% CI 0.398-1.448)
  • Only 34.2% of patients reported high-quality SDM overall, regardless of modality
  • Educational materials provided significantly less often in telehealth consultations (22.6% vs 40.6%, p=0.008)
Clinical Relevance: Supports telehealth as an equivalent alternative for lung cancer screening discussions, potentially improving access to screening programmes across geographically dispersed NHS populations.
Limitations: Low response rate (31%) and single healthcare system (US Veterans Affairs) limits generalisability to UK NHS settings.
PubMed →DOI →
Chest

Dupilumab Reduces Mucus Radiodensity and New Plug Formation in Asthma

Sandhu E, Ragunayakam N, Mappanasingam AA et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Prospective controlled study
Key Question: Does dupilumab modify the volume, radiodensity, and temporal behaviour of individual CT-visible mucus plugs in moderate-to-severe asthma?
Key Findings:
  • Dupilumab significantly increased mucus plug resolution (91% vs 58% in controls, p<0.0001) and reduced new plug formation (median 1 vs 6 plugs, p=0.0008)
  • Complete mucus plug resolution occurred in 36% of dupilumab patients vs 10% of controls
  • Persistent plugs showed reduced radiodensity post-dupilumab (-422HU to -569HU, p=0.009), correlating with improved FEV1, FEV1/FVC, and ventilation defects
Clinical Relevance: This provides objective CT evidence supporting dupilumab's mucus-modifying effects in moderate-to-severe asthma, offering NHS clinicians quantitative markers to assess treatment response beyond standard spirometry.
Limitations: Small sample size (32 participants) and relatively short follow-up period (16 weeks minimum) limit generalisability of findings.
PubMed →DOI →
Chest

Impact of Antifibrotic Adherence and Dosing on Risk of Mortality and Hospitalization in Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis: A Nested Case-Control Study

Xu H, Meier E, Chhabria MS et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Nested case-control study
Key Question: How do antifibrotic adherence and dose reduction affect mortality and hospitalisation risk in IPF patients?
Key Findings:
  • Good adherence (≥75% of days covered) reduced mortality risk by 44% (OR=0.563, p<0.001) and hospitalisation risk by 31% (OR=0.692, p=0.016)
  • Dose reduction increased mortality risk by 57% (OR=1.57, p=0.024) and hospitalisation risk by 67% (OR=1.667, p=0.008), but only for nintedanib users
  • Pirfenidone showed no association between adherence/dosing and hospitalisation outcomes
Clinical Relevance: This supports maintaining standard antifibrotic dosing where tolerated in NHS IPF services, particularly for nintedanib, and emphasises the importance of adherence support strategies.
Limitations: Administrative database design cannot account for clinical reasons for dose reduction or treatment discontinuation.
PubMed →DOI →
Chest

Meconium Aspiration Syndrome and the Association with Preschool Asthma

Gatt D, Ben-Shitrit I, Golan-Tripto I et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does meconium aspiration syndrome (MAS) in the neonatal period associate with subsequent development of preschool asthma?
Key Findings:
  • Among 326,940 children, MAS affected 0.2% (730 infants) and was associated with 64% higher risk of asthma by age 6 years (95% CI 1.29-2.06, p<0.001)
  • MAS survivors had increased rates of bronchiolitis and obstructive sleep apnea compared to matched controls
  • Atopic conditions (dermatitis, rhinitis, food allergies) showed no significant difference between groups
Clinical Relevance: This suggests neonatal respiratory teams should flag MAS survivors for enhanced respiratory surveillance, potentially informing early intervention strategies and parental counselling about future asthma risk in UK paediatric services.
Limitations: Retrospective design limits ability to establish causation and may be subject to unmeasured confounding factors.
PubMed →DOI →
Chest

Phylogenomic and clinical perspectives of an East Asia-specific cluster of Mycobacterium massiliense

Kim DH, Jung S, Jung S et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study with genomic analysis
Key Question: What are the epidemiological characteristics and clinical significance of sequence type 120 (ST120) Mycobacterium abscessus subspecies massiliense in East Asia?
Key Findings:
  • ST120 comprised 36.8% of isolates in South Korea, with geographic restriction to East Asia (South Korea, Japan, Taiwan) and evidence of stepwise transmission between countries
  • Patients with ST120 infections had significantly higher rates of cavitary disease (46% vs 22.1%, P=0.004) and lower microbiological cure rates (40.0% vs 70.6%, P=0.043)
  • All ST120 strains showed rough colony morphotype with deletions in glycopeptidolipid biosynthesis genes and virulence-associated mutations in the ESX system
Clinical Relevance: This identifies a geographically restricted, more virulent strain of MAM that UK clinicians may encounter in patients with East Asian travel history or ethnicity, potentially requiring more aggressive treatment approaches.
Limitations: Single-centre study from South Korea may not reflect broader clinical patterns across different healthcare systems.
PubMed →DOI →
Chest

A contemporary approach to diagnosis and treatment of right heart thrombus associated with pulmonary embolism

Matusov Y, Dohad S, Megna D et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Commentary/clinical review
Key Question: How should clinicians diagnose and manage right heart thrombus in patients with pulmonary embolism?
Key Findings:
  • Right heart thrombus represents a high-risk complication of PE associated with increased mortality and clinical deterioration
  • Management options include surgical embolectomy, systemic thrombolysis, catheter-based mechanical thrombectomy, or anticoagulation alone in selected patients
  • Current practice relies primarily on case series and anecdotal evidence rather than rigorous clinical trial data
Clinical Relevance: This provides UK respiratory and acute medicine clinicians with a practical framework for managing right heart thrombus, a high-risk PE complication not addressed in current guidelines or risk stratification tools.
Limitations: Limited to existing case series and observational data without robust clinical trial evidence to guide treatment decisions.
PubMed →DOI →
